---
title: "Beyond GLMs with splines"
institute: "University of Bayreuth"
author: "Bert van der Veen"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    toc: false
    slide_level: 2
    latex_engine: lualatex
    includes:
      in_header: ../header.tex
header-includes:
  - \AtBeginSection{}
  - \useoutertheme{miniframes}
  - \setbeamertemplate{frametitle}{\vskip0.3cm\usebeamerfont*{frametitle}\insertframetitle\vskip-1.5ex\begin{beamercolorbox}[colsep=0.2pt, wd=\textwidth]{lower separation line head}\end{beamercolorbox}}
  - \setbeamercolor{lower separation line head}{bg=orange}
  - \definecolor{shadecolor}{RGB}{200, 200, 200}
  - \hypersetup{colorlinks,citecolor=orange,filecolor=red,linkcolor=brown,urlcolor=blue}
  - \definecolor{green}{RGB}{48, 69, 41}
  - \definecolor{darkorange}{RGB}{255, 150, 0}
  - \definecolor{gray}{RGB}{100, 100, 100}
  - \setbeamercolor{itemize item}{fg=orange}
  - \setbeamercolor{itemize subitem}{fg=orange}
  - \setbeamercolor{enumerate item}{fg=orange}
  - \setbeamercolor{enumerate subitem}{fg=orange}
  - \tcbuselibrary{skins}
  - \usepackage{emoji}
  - \usepackage{longtable,booktabs}
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
urlcolor: orange
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(knitr)

default_source_hook <- knit_hooks$get('source')
default_output_hook <- knit_hooks$get('output')

knit_hooks$set(
  source = function(x, options) {
    paste0(
      "\n::: {.codebox data-latex=\"\"}\n\n",
      default_source_hook(x, options),
      "\n\n:::\n\n")
  }
)

knit_hooks$set(
  output = function(x, options) {
    paste0(
      "\n::: {.codebox data-latex=\"\"}\n\n",
      default_output_hook(x, options),
      "\n\n:::\n\n")
  }
)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Outline Today

- Ridge regression
- Generalized Additive Models
- Penalized splines
- Smoothing parameter selection
- GAMs as GLMMs
- Example

- 15 minute break
- 1 Lecture/presentation
- 1 Practical

## Questions about yesterday?

\center

![](questions.jpg){width=40%}

## Resources

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Fieberg, J.} (2024). \textit{Statistics for Ecologists.} \newline
\url{https://statistics4ecologists-v3.netlify.app} \newline
\footnotesize Ch. 4: Non-linear models — intuitive treatment of splines and basis functions \normalsize

\item \textbf{Simpson, G.} Physalia GAM course. \newline
\url{https://github.com/gavinsimpson/physalia-gam-course} \newline
\footnotesize Comprehensive practical course with \texttt{mgcv}; slides and code freely available \normalsize

\item \textbf{Wood, S.N.} (2017). \textit{Generalized Additive Models: An Introduction with R}, 2nd ed. Chapman \& Hall. \newline
\footnotesize The definitive reference; covers all theory and \texttt{mgcv} in depth \normalsize

\end{itemize}

## \texttt{R} packages for GAMs

\columnsbegin
\column{0.5\textwidth}

\textbf{Fitting}
\begin{itemize}
\item \texttt{mgcv} — the standard; most features, best documented
\item \texttt{gamm4} — GAMMs via \texttt{lme4} backend (Gaussian)
\item \texttt{glmmTMB} — GAM smooths + arbitrary GLMM structure
\end{itemize}

\column{0.5\textwidth}

\textbf{Visualisation \& diagnostics}
\begin{itemize}
\item \texttt{gratia} — \texttt{ggplot2}-based smooth plots, \texttt{draw()}, \texttt{appraise()}
\item \texttt{DHARMa} — simulation residuals (works with \texttt{mgcv})
\item \texttt{ggeffects} — marginal predictions for \texttt{mgcv} models
\end{itemize}

\columnsend

\center

\textbf{We will use \texttt{mgcv} for fitting and \texttt{gratia} for visualisation}

# Recap

## Where we are

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
\textbf{Model} & \textbf{Non-linear covariates} & \textbf{Correlation} \\
\midrule
GLM & No & No \\
GLMM & No & Yes (random effects) \\
\textcolor{orange}{GAM} & \textcolor{orange}{Yes (smooth functions)} & \textcolor{orange}{Optional} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\pause

- GLMs and GLMMs do have non-linearity through the link function, but assume covariates enter the linear predictor **linearly**
- Many ecological relationships are non-linear

## Non-linearity in ecology

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
ISIT <- read.csv("../data/ISIT.csv")
dat <- subset(ISIT, Station == 9)
par(mfrow=c(1,2), mar=c(5,5,3,2))
plot(Sources ~ SampleDepth, data = dat, pch=16, col="steelblue",
     xlab="Depth (m)", ylab="Bioluminescent sources",
     main="Bioluminescence vs. depth", cex=1.5, cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5)
m_glm <- glm(Sources ~ SampleDepth, family=Gamma(link="log"), data=dat)
lines(dat$SampleDepth[order(dat$SampleDepth)],
      predict(m_glm, type="response")[order(dat$SampleDepth)],
      col="red", lwd=2)
legend("topright", lty=1, col="red", legend="Poisson GLM", cex=1.3)

plot(residuals(m_glm, type="deviance") ~ fitted(m_glm),
     pch=16, col="steelblue", xlab="Fitted values", ylab="Deviance residuals",
     main="GLM residuals", cex=1.5, cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5)
abline(h=0, lty="dashed")
lines(loess.smooth(fitted(m_glm), residuals(m_glm, type="deviance")), col="red", lwd=2)
```

A Poisson GLM misses the non-linear relationship.

# Ridge Regression

## Penalized regression

Ordinary least squares minimizes:

\begin{equation}
\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \lVert \textbf{y} - \textbf{X}\symbf{\beta} \rVert^2
\end{equation}

\pause

**Ridge regression** \tiny (Hoerl and Kennard 1970) \normalsize adds a penalty on the size of the coefficients:

\begin{equation}
\lVert \textbf{y} - \textbf{X}\symbf{\beta} \rVert^2 + \lambda \lVert \symbf{\beta} \rVert^2
\end{equation}

- $\lambda \geq 0$ controls how strongly coefficients are penalized towards zero
- $\lambda = 0$: ordinary least squares
- $\lambda \to \infty$: all $\hat{\beta}_j \to 0$

## Ridge regression: solution

The ridge estimator has a closed form:

\begin{equation}
\hat{\symbf{\beta}}_\text{ridge} = (\textbf{X}^\top\textbf{X} + \lambda\textbf{I})^{-1}\textbf{X}^\top\textbf{y}
\end{equation}

\pause

\begin{itemize}
\item The $\lambda\textbf{I}$ term regularizes the problem — helps when $\textbf{X}^\top\textbf{X}$ is ill-conditioned
\item Ridge regression \textbf{shrinks} estimates toward zero
\item Very useful in high-dimensional settings (more predictors than observations)
\end{itemize}

## Ridge regression as a GLMM

Ridge regression is equivalent to a mixed model with:

\begin{equation}
\symbf{\beta} \sim \mathcal{N}\biggl(\textbf{0},\, \frac{\sigma^2}{\lambda}\,\textbf{I}\biggr)
\end{equation}

\pause

For a Gaussian model with \textbf{known} $\lambda$: minimizing the penalized sum of squares gives the posterior mode (MAP estimate) of $\symbf{\beta}$ — the conditional mode given the data.

\pause

\begin{center}
\textbf{The penalty $\lambda$ controls variance: small $\lambda$ $\Leftrightarrow$ large variance $\Leftrightarrow$ flexible}
\end{center}

\pause

\textcolor{orange}{GAMs extend this idea: instead of penalizing the size of $\symbf{\beta}$, we penalize the \textbf{wiggliness} of a smooth function.}

## Ridge via \texttt{mgcv}: a code example

Road kills data — Poisson response, multiple continuous predictors

```{r ridge_mgcv, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, cache=TRUE}
library(mgcv)
rk <- read.csv("../data/RoadKills.csv")

# Unpenalized Poisson GLM
m_glm <- glm(BufoCalamita ~ D.WAT.RES + D.WAT.COUR + L.D.ROAD,
             family=poisson, data=rk)

# Ridge via mgcv: paraPen adds lambda * beta' I beta
# to the penalized log-likelihood (shared lambda for all 3)
# Scale first so penalty is on comparable scale
Xblock <- scale(as.matrix(rk[, c("D.WAT.RES","D.WAT.COUR","L.D.ROAD")]))
rk$Xblock <- Xblock
m_ridge <- gam(BufoCalamita ~ Xblock,
               family=poisson, data=rk,
               paraPen=list(Xblock=list(diag(3))),
               method="REML")
```

## Ridge via \texttt{mgcv}: comparison to GLMM

```{r ridge_compare, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, cache=TRUE}
# GLMM: same shrinkage, different syntax
library(glmmTMB)
n <- nrow(rk)
dat_long <- data.frame(
  y    = rep(rk$BufoCalamita, 3),
  xval = c(Xblock),
  pred = factor(rep(c("D.WAT.RES","D.WAT.COUR","L.D.ROAD"), each=n)))
m_glmm <- glmmTMB(y ~ (0+xval|pred), family=poisson, data=dat_long)

b_glm   <- coef(m_glm)[-1]
b_ridge <- coef(m_ridge)[-1]
b_glmm  <- ranef(m_glmm)$cond$pred[,"xval"]
```

```{r ridge_plot, echo=FALSE, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=10, cache=TRUE}
par(mar=c(5,5,3,2), mfrow=c(1,2))
plot(b_glm, b_ridge, pch=16, col="steelblue", cex=2,
     xlab="GLM estimate (unpenalized)", ylab="mgcv ridge (paraPen, REML)",
     main="Ridge shrinks toward zero", cex.lab=1.4, cex.main=1.3)
abline(0,1,lty="dashed"); abline(h=0,v=0,col="gray")
text(b_glm, b_ridge, labels=c("D.WAT.RES","D.WAT.COUR","L.D.ROAD"),
     pos=3, cex=0.9)
plot(b_ridge, b_glmm, pch=16, col="steelblue", cex=2,
     xlab="mgcv ridge (paraPen)", ylab="glmmTMB BLUP",
     main="mgcv and glmmTMB agree", cex.lab=1.4, cex.main=1.3)
abline(0,1,lty="dashed")
text(b_ridge, b_glmm, labels=c("D.WAT.RES","D.WAT.COUR","L.D.ROAD"),
     pos=3, cex=0.9)
```

## The distinction does not quite hold for GLMMs

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Ridge}: $\lambda$ is fixed externally (cross-validation, AIC); $\symbf{\beta}$ is optimized directly — no marginal likelihood
\pause
\item \textbf{GLMM}: variance components (including the ratio $\sigma^2/\sigma^2_b = \lambda$) are estimated by \textbf{marginalizing over} the random effects via ML or REML; the BLUP is then computed conditionally on those estimates
\pause
\item \textbf{Non-Gaussian GLMMs}: the posterior $p(\textbf{u}\vert\textbf{y})$ is not Gaussian, so posterior mode $\neq$ posterior mean; the BLUP (posterior mean) requires integration, not just optimization
\pause
\item The equivalence holds \textbf{exactly} only for Gaussian models with known variance components
\end{itemize}

\pause

\begin{center}
\textcolor{orange}{The analogy is still useful for intuition, but the mechanics differ}
\end{center}

# What is a GAM?

## GAM formulation

A Generalized Additive Model replaces linear predictors with smooth functions:

\begin{equation}
g\{\text{E}(y_i \vert \textbf{x}_i)\} = \alpha + f_1(x_{i1}) + f_2(x_{i2}) + \cdots
\end{equation}

\columnsbegin
\column{0.5\textwidth}

**GLM:**
$$g\{\text{E}(y_i)\} = \alpha + x_{i1}\beta_1 + x_{i2}\beta_2$$

\column{0.5\textwidth}

**GAM:**
$$g\{\text{E}(y_i)\} = \alpha + f_1(x_{i1}) + f_2(x_{i2})$$

\columnsend

\pause

- $f_j(\cdot)$ are smooth, flexible functions estimated from data
- Linear terms $x\beta$ are a special case
- Random effects can be included: $g\{\text{E}(y_i \vert u_j)\} = \alpha + f(x_i) + u_j$

## Why "additive"?

Each smooth contributes **additively** to the linear predictor — just as in a GLM.

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(mgcv)
set.seed(1)
n <- 200
x1 <- runif(n)
x2 <- runif(n)
y <- sin(2*pi*x1) + exp(x2) + rnorm(n, sd=0.3)
dat2 <- data.frame(x1=x1, x2=x2, y=y)
m2 <- gam(y ~ s(x1) + s(x2), data=dat2)
par(mfrow=c(1,2), mar=c(5,5,3,2))
plot(m2, select=1, shade=TRUE, col="orange", cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, main="f(x1)", cex.main=1.5)
plot(m2, select=2, shade=TRUE, col="orange", cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, main="f(x2)", cex.main=1.5)
```

Each smooth is estimated **while holding all others constant** — the partial effect.

# Splines

## What is a spline?

A spline is a **piecewise polynomial** — multiple polynomial segments joined smoothly at **knots** $\xi_1, \ldots, \xi_K$

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=4.5, fig.width=13}
set.seed(42)
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out=200)
knots <- c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)
par(mfrow=c(1,2), mar=c(5,5,3,2))

# Piecewise linear (discontinuous at knots)
y_pw <- ifelse(x < 0.25, 0.5 + 2*x,
        ifelse(x < 0.5,  -0.2 + 4*(x-0.25),
        ifelse(x < 0.75, 0.8 - 2*(x-0.5), 0.3 + x)))
plot(x, y_pw, type="l", lwd=2, col="steelblue",
     xlab="x", ylab="f(x)", main="Piecewise polynomial", cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5)
abline(v=knots, lty="dashed", col="gray")
text(knots, par("usr")[3]+0.05, labels=expression(xi[1], xi[2], xi[3]), cex=1.3, col="gray40")

# Smooth spline through same data
y_sp <- sin(2*pi*x*0.8) * 0.4 + 0.5
plot(x, y_sp, type="l", lwd=2, col="orange",
     xlab="x", ylab="f(x)", main="Smooth spline", cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5)
abline(v=knots, lty="dashed", col="gray")
text(knots, par("usr")[3]+0.02, labels=expression(xi[1], xi[2], xi[3]), cex=1.3, col="gray40")
```

A smooth spline constrains adjacent segments to join with **continuous derivatives** — no kinks.

## Basis functions: building blocks

Any smooth $f(x)$ can be represented as a **weighted sum of basis functions** $b_j(x)$:

\begin{equation}
f(x) = \sum_{j=1}^K \beta_j\, b_j(x) = \textbf{b}(x)^\top\symbf{\beta}
\end{equation}

\pause

This is the same $\textbf{X}\symbf{\beta}$ structure as a GLM — we simply \textbf{redefine the design matrix} $\textbf{X}$ to contain basis function columns:

\begin{equation}
\textbf{X}\symbf{\beta}, \quad \text{where } X_{ij} = b_j(x_i)
\end{equation}

\pause

\begin{itemize}
\item No new estimation machinery needed
\item Penalization is the only addition
\item The same link functions and distributions apply
\end{itemize}

## Cubic regression splines

The cubic regression spline (CRS) basis uses cubic polynomials between knots, constrained to have continuous first and second derivatives at knots.

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(mgcv)
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out=200)
sm <- smoothCon(s(x, bs="cr", k=7), data=data.frame(x=x), knots=NULL)[[1]]
B <- sm$X
par(mar=c(5,5,3,2))
matplot(x, B, type="l", lty=1, lwd=2,
        xlab="x", ylab="Basis function value",
        main="Cubic regression spline basis (k = 7)",
        cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5,
        col=c("steelblue","orange","forestgreen","red","purple","brown","pink"))
abline(h=0, lty="dashed", col="gray")
```

Each basis function has **local support** — it is non-zero only near its knot.

## Thin plate regression splines

The \textbf{default in \texttt{mgcv}} \tiny (Wood 2003) \normalsize. A TPS finds the function $f$ minimising:

\begin{equation}
\sum_{i=1}^n \{y_i - f(x_i)\}^2 + \lambda \int \{f''(x)\}^2\,dx
\end{equation}

over \textbf{all} smooth functions — no knots to choose. The solution is a natural spline with knots at every data point. For large $n$, \texttt{mgcv} uses a low-rank approximation: keep only the $k$ leading eigenfunctions of the penalty operator.

\pause

\textbf{P-splines} \tiny (Eilers and Marx 1996)\normalsize: B-spline basis + difference penalty on adjacent coefficients

\textbf{Cyclic cubic splines} (\texttt{bs="cc"}): smooth joins at endpoints — for periodic predictors (month, day of year)

## Spline summary

\begin{center}
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
\textbf{Type} & \textbf{mgcv code} & \textbf{When to use} \\
\midrule
Thin plate (default) & \texttt{s(x)} & General purpose \\
Cubic regression & \texttt{s(x, bs="cr")} & Interpretable, fast \\
P-spline & \texttt{s(x, bs="ps")} & Large datasets \\
Cyclic cubic & \texttt{s(x, bs="cc")} & Periodic predictors \\
Duchon spline & \texttt{s(x, bs="ds")} & Spatial data \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

## Building a smooth: step by step

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, out.height="0.65\\textheight"}
set.seed(7)
n <- 120
x <- sort(runif(n))
y <- sin(2*pi*x) + rnorm(n, sd=0.4)
dat3 <- data.frame(x=x, y=y)
m3 <- gam(y ~ s(x, bs="cr", k=6), data=dat3)

sm3 <- smoothCon(s(x, bs="cr", k=6), data=dat3)[[1]]
B3 <- sm3$X
cf <- coef(m3)[-1]

par(mfrow=c(1,3), mar=c(5,5,3,2))

# Step 1: basis functions
matplot(x, B3, type="l", lty=1, lwd=2,
        xlab="x", ylab="", main="1. Basis functions",
        cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.2, cex.main=1.5,
        col=c("steelblue","orange","forestgreen","red","purple","brown"))

# Step 2: weighted basis functions
matplot(x, sweep(B3, 2, cf, "*"), type="l", lty=1, lwd=2,
        xlab="x", ylab="", main="2. Weighted by coefficients",
        cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.2, cex.main=1.5,
        col=c("steelblue","orange","forestgreen","red","purple","brown"))
abline(h=0, lty="dashed", col="gray")

# Step 3: sum = smooth
plot(x, y, pch=16, col="gray60", xlab="x", ylab="", main="3. Sum = smooth",
     cex=1.2, cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.2, cex.main=1.5)
lines(x, fitted(m3) - coef(m3)[1], col="orange", lwd=3)
```

# Penalization

## Wiggliness penalty

Without a penalty, enough basis functions can fit *any* dataset perfectly (overfitting).

\pause

The **wiggliness penalty** discourages overly complex smooths:

\begin{equation}
\text{PRSS} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n \{y_i - f(x_i)\}^2}_{\text{Fit to data}} + \lambda \underbrace{\int \{f''(x)\}^2\, dx}_{\text{Wiggliness}}
\end{equation}

\pause

- $f''(x)$ is the second derivative — measures curvature
- $\lambda \geq 0$ is the **smoothing parameter**
- $\lambda = 0$: interpolating spline (overfits)
- $\lambda \to \infty$: straight line (linear regression)

## Smoothing parameter $\lambda$

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5.5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
set.seed(3)
n <- 80
x <- sort(runif(n))
y <- sin(2*pi*x) + rnorm(n, sd=0.3)
dat4 <- data.frame(x=x, y=y)
xp <- seq(0, 1, length.out=200)
datp <- data.frame(x=xp)

par(mfrow=c(1,3), mar=c(5,5,3,2))
spans <- c(0.08, 0.35, 0.9)
mains <- list(expression(paste("Small ", lambda, " (wiggly)")),
              expression(paste("Medium ", lambda)),
              expression(paste("Large ", lambda, " (smooth)")))
for(i in seq_along(spans)){
  m_sp <- loess(y ~ x, data=dat4, span=spans[i])
  plot(x, y, pch=16, col="gray60",
       xlab="x", ylab="f(x)",
       main=mains[[i]],
       cex=1.3, cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5)
  lines(xp, predict(m_sp, newdata=datp), col="orange", lwd=3)
}
```

The same data; only $\lambda$ differs.

## The penalty in matrix form

For a penalized spline with basis matrix $\textbf{B}$ and coefficients $\symbf{\beta}$:

\begin{equation}
\text{PRSS} = \lVert \textbf{y} - \textbf{B}\symbf{\beta} \rVert^2 + \lambda\, \symbf{\beta}^\top \textbf{S}\, \symbf{\beta}
\end{equation}

- $\textbf{S}$ is the **penalty matrix** encoding the wiggliness constraint
- For a cubic spline: $S_{jk} = \int b_j''(x)\, b_k''(x)\, dx$

\pause

The solution is:

\begin{equation}
\hat{\symbf{\beta}} = (\textbf{B}^\top\textbf{B} + \lambda\textbf{S})^{-1}\textbf{B}^\top\textbf{y}
\end{equation}

\pause

Compare to ridge: $(\textbf{X}^\top\textbf{X} + \lambda\textbf{I})^{-1}\textbf{X}^\top\textbf{y}$ — same structure, $\textbf{S}$ replaces $\textbf{I}$

## Effective degrees of freedom

The effective degrees of freedom (EDF) of a smooth quantifies its complexity:

\begin{equation}
\text{EDF} = \text{tr}\bigl\{(\textbf{B}^\top\textbf{B} + \lambda\textbf{S})^{-1}\textbf{B}^\top\textbf{B}\bigr\}
\end{equation}

\columnsbegin
\column{0.5\textwidth}

- EDF = 1: straight line
- EDF = 2: quadratic
- EDF $\approx k$: nearly interpolating

\column{0.5\textwidth}

- Reported by \texttt{summary.gam}
- Used for hypothesis tests
- Larger EDF $\Rightarrow$ more complex smooth

\columnsend

# Smoothing parameter selection

## How do we choose $\lambda$?

Several criteria exist for selecting the smoothing parameter automatically:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{GCV} --- Generalized Cross-Validation
\item \textbf{ML} --- Maximum Likelihood
\item \textbf{REML} --- Restricted Maximum Likelihood
\end{enumerate}

All treat $\lambda$ as something to be estimated from the data.

## GCV: Generalized Cross-Validation

Proposed by Craven and Wahba (1979), popularized for GAMs by \textbf{Wood (2004)}:

\begin{equation}
\text{GCV}(\lambda) = \frac{n \cdot \text{RSS}(\lambda)}{\{n - \text{EDF}(\lambda)\}^2}
\end{equation}

\pause

\begin{itemize}
\item Approximates leave-one-out cross-validation at $O(n)$ cost
\item Penalizes model complexity via EDF
\item Minimizing GCV over $\lambda$ balances fit vs. smoothness
\end{itemize}

\pause

\textcolor{red}{\textbf{Problem}: GCV can undersmooth (too wiggly) in some cases, especially with correlated errors \tiny (Reiss and Ogden 2009)\normalsize}

## ML and REML

The GLMM representation of the smooth (see next section) enables likelihood-based selection:

\pause

**ML** (Maximum Likelihood): maximize the marginal likelihood of $\textbf{y}$ over both $\symbf{\beta}$ and $\lambda$

\pause

**REML** (Restricted Maximum Likelihood): maximize the likelihood after projecting out fixed effects

\begin{itemize}
\item REML is less prone to undersmoothing than ML \tiny (Wood 2011)\normalsize
\item REML accounts for uncertainty in fixed effects when estimating $\lambda$
\item Generally recommended; default in \texttt{mgcv} since version 1.8
\end{itemize}

\pause

\begin{center}
\textbf{Use \texttt{method="REML"} in \texttt{mgcv::gam()} (it is the default)}
\end{center}

## Comparing criteria

\begin{center}
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{llll}
\toprule
\textbf{Criterion} & \textbf{Basis} & \textbf{Tends to} & \textbf{Use when} \\
\midrule
GCV & CV approximation & Undersmooth & Legacy, simple models \\
ML & Likelihood & Undersmooth & Rarely preferred \\
REML & Restricted likelihood & Best calibrated & Default (recommended) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\pause

In \texttt{R}:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
gam(y ~ s(x), method = "REML")  # default, recommended
gam(y ~ s(x), method = "GCV.Cp") # GCV
gam(y ~ s(x), method = "ML")     # ML
```

# GAMs as GLMMs

## The GLMM representation

\tiny (Wood 2004, 2011) \normalsize

The penalty matrix $\textbf{S}$ is rank-deficient: it has a **null space** (directions that are not penalized).

\pause

Decompose $\textbf{S}$ via spectral decomposition $\textbf{S} = \textbf{U}\symbf{\Lambda}\textbf{U}^\top$ and partition the smooth:

\begin{equation}
f(x) = \underbrace{\textbf{B}_0\symbf{\beta}_0}_{\text{Unpenalized (fixed)}} + \underbrace{\textbf{B}_1\textbf{b}}_{\text{Penalized (random)}}
\end{equation}

\pause

with $\textbf{b} \sim \mathcal{N}\bigl(\textbf{0},\, \frac{\sigma^2}{\lambda}\textbf{I}\bigr)$

\pause

This is **exactly** a GLMM: fixed-effects design matrix $\textbf{X} = \textbf{B}_0$ and random-effects design matrix $\textbf{Z} = \textbf{B}_1$.

## What this means in practice

\begin{itemize}
\item GAMs can be fitted using GLMM software
\item The smoothing parameter $\lambda$ is determined by the random-effect variance: $\sigma^2_b = \sigma^2/\lambda$
\item REML estimation of $\lambda$ is exactly REML estimation of the random-effect variance
\item GAMs and GLMMs are \textbf{two sides of the same coin}
\end{itemize}

\pause

\begin{center}
\textbf{A GAM is a GLMM with specially structured random effects}
\end{center}

## GAMs in \texttt{glmmTMB}

Since the GAM smooth is a GLMM, it can be fitted in \texttt{glmmTMB} using \texttt{mgcv} smooth terms:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
library(glmmTMB)
model <- glmmTMB(y ~ s(x), family = poisson, data = dat)
```

\pause

You can combine GAM smooths with random effects in the same model:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
model <- glmmTMB(y ~ s(x) + (1|group),
                 family = poisson, data = dat)
```

\pause

\begin{itemize}
\item Uses the Wood (2004, 2011) diagonalization internally
\item Supports all \texttt{mgcv} basis types
\item Enables non-Gaussian responses with GAM-type smoothing
\end{itemize}

# Example

## Example: bioluminescence at depth

Data from Gillibrand et al. (2007), via Zuur et al. (2009)

\columnsbegin
\column{0.5\textwidth}

- 1,654 observations across 19 stations
- Response: number of bioluminescent sources
- Predictor: sample depth (m)
- Strong non-linear relationship with depth

\column{0.5\textwidth}

```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.height=4.5}
ISIT <- read.csv("../data/ISIT.csv")
dat_ex <- subset(ISIT, Station == 9)
plot(Sources ~ SampleDepth, data = dat_ex,
     pch=16, col="steelblue", cex=1.5,
     xlab="Depth (m)", ylab="Sources",
     cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3)
```

\columnsend

## ISIT: GLM vs. GAM

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5.5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(mgcv)
dat_ex <- subset(ISIT, Station == 9)
xseq <- seq(min(dat_ex$SampleDepth), max(dat_ex$SampleDepth), length.out=300)
pred_df <- data.frame(SampleDepth = xseq)

m_glm <- glm(Sources ~ SampleDepth, family=Gamma(link="log"), data=dat_ex)
m_gam <- gam(Sources ~ s(SampleDepth, bs="cr"), family=Gamma(link="log"),
             data=dat_ex, method="REML")

par(mfrow=c(1,2), mar=c(5,5,3,2))
plot(Sources ~ SampleDepth, data=dat_ex, pch=16, col="gray60",
     xlab="Depth (m)", ylab="Sources", main="GLM vs. GAM",
     cex=1.3, cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5)
lines(xseq, predict(m_glm, newdata=pred_df, type="response"),
      col="red", lwd=2, lty="dashed")
lines(xseq, predict(m_gam, newdata=pred_df, type="response"),
      col="orange", lwd=3)
legend("topright", lty=c("dashed","solid"), col=c("red","orange"),
       legend=c("Poisson GLM","Poisson GAM"), lwd=2, cex=1.2)

plot(m_gam, shade=TRUE, col="orange", shift=coef(m_gam)[1],
     trans=exp, xlab="Depth (m)", ylab="Sources",
     main="GAM smooth with 95% CI",
     cex.lab=1.5, cex.axis=1.3, cex.main=1.5)
points(dat_ex$SampleDepth, dat_ex$Sources, pch=16, col="gray60", cex=1.3)
```

## ISIT: fitting

```{r, echo=TRUE, results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(mgcv)
ISIT <- read.csv("../data/ISIT.csv")
dat_ex <- subset(ISIT, Station == 9)

model_gam <- gam(Sources ~ s(SampleDepth, bs="cr"),
                 family = Gamma(link="log"), data = dat_ex,
                 method = "REML")
```

## ISIT: summary

\footnotesize
```{r, echo=FALSE}
summary(model_gam)
```
\normalsize

## ISIT: model checking

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, out.height="0.65\\textheight", results='hide'}
par(mfrow=c(2,2), mar=c(4,4,3,1))
gam.check(model_gam, cex.lab=1.2, cex.axis=1.1)
```

## ISIT: multiple stations

\footnotesize

```{r, eval=FALSE}
model_full <- gam(Sources ~ s(SampleDepth, bs="cr") +
                    s(Station, bs="re"),
                  family = Gamma(link="log"), data = ISIT,
                  method = "REML")
```

\normalsize

- \texttt{s(Station, bs="re")}: random intercept for each station — a GAMM
- The same in \texttt{glmmTMB}:

\footnotesize

```{r, eval=FALSE}
library(glmmTMB)
model_tmb <- glmmTMB(Sources ~ s(SampleDepth, bs="cr") +
                       (1|Station),
                     family = Gamma(link="log"), data = ISIT)
```

\normalsize

# Example 2: road kills

## Road-killed toads in Portugal

Data from Zuur et al. (2009), originally Mira et al.

\columnsbegin
\column{0.5\textwidth}

- 52 road sectors in Portugal
- Response: number of road-killed *Bufo calamita* (natterjack toad)
- Predictor: distance to nearest water reservoir (`D.WAT.RES`)
- Non-linear relationship expected — toads concentrate near water

\column{0.5\textwidth}

```{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.height=4.5}
rk <- read.csv("../data/RoadKills.csv")
plot(BufoCalamita ~ D.WAT.RES, data=rk,
     pch=16, col="steelblue", cex=1.3,
     xlab="Distance to water reservoir (m)",
     ylab="Road kills", cex.lab=1.4, cex.axis=1.2)
```

\columnsend

## Road kills: GLM vs. GAM

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5.5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(mgcv)
xrng <- range(rk$D.WAT.RES)
xseq <- seq(xrng[1], xrng[2], length.out=300)
pdat <- data.frame(D.WAT.RES=xseq)

m_rk_glm <- glm(BufoCalamita ~ D.WAT.RES, family=poisson, data=rk)
m_rk_gam <- gam(BufoCalamita ~ s(D.WAT.RES, bs="cr"),
                family=poisson, data=rk, method="REML")

par(mfrow=c(1,2), mar=c(5,5,3,2))
plot(BufoCalamita ~ D.WAT.RES, data=rk, pch=16, col="gray60",
     xlab="Distance to water (m)", ylab="Road kills",
     main="GLM vs. GAM", cex=1.3, cex.lab=1.4, cex.axis=1.2, cex.main=1.4)
lines(xseq, predict(m_rk_glm, newdata=pdat, type="response"),
      col="red", lwd=2, lty="dashed")
lines(xseq, predict(m_rk_gam, newdata=pdat, type="response"),
      col="orange", lwd=3)
legend("topright", lty=c("dashed","solid"), col=c("red","orange"),
       legend=c("Poisson GLM","Poisson GAM"), lwd=2, cex=1.1)

plot(m_rk_gam, shade=TRUE, col="orange", shift=coef(m_rk_gam)[1],
     trans=exp, xlab="Distance to water (m)", ylab="Road kills",
     main="GAM smooth with 95% CI",
     cex.lab=1.4, cex.axis=1.2, cex.main=1.4)
points(rk$D.WAT.RES, rk$BufoCalamita, pch=16, col="gray60", cex=1.3)
```

## Road kills: fitting

```{r, echo=TRUE, results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
rk <- read.csv("../data/RoadKills.csv")

model_rk <- gam(BufoCalamita ~ s(D.WAT.RES, bs="cr"),
                family = poisson, data = rk, method = "REML")
```

## Road kills: summary

\footnotesize
```{r, echo=FALSE}
summary(model_rk)
```
\normalsize

## Road kills: model checking

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.height=5, fig.width=13, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, out.height="0.65\\textheight", results='hide'}
par(mfrow=c(2,2), mar=c(4,4,3,1))
gam.check(model_rk, cex.lab=1.2, cex.axis=1.1)
```

## Road kills: visualisation with \texttt{gratia}

```{r, echo=TRUE, fig.height=5, fig.width=10, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(gratia)
draw(model_rk, residuals=TRUE)
```

## Road kills: adding more predictors

\footnotesize
```{r, eval=FALSE}
model_rk2 <- gam(BufoCalamita ~ s(D.WAT.RES, bs="cr") +
                   s(D.WAT.COUR, bs="cr") +
                   s(L.D.ROAD, bs="cr"),
                 family = poisson, data = rk, method = "REML")
```
\normalsize

- Multiple smooths can be combined additively
- Each smooth's edf is estimated and penalized independently
- Use AIC or REML score to compare models

# Summary

## Summary

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Ridge regression}: penalizes $\lVert\symbf{\beta}\rVert^2$ — equivalent to a normal prior; conditional mode = MAP estimate (not a BLUP)
\item \textbf{GAMs} extend GLMs with smooth functions $f(x)$ instead of linear terms $x\beta$
\item \textbf{Splines} represent $f(x)$ as weighted basis functions; many types available
\item \textbf{Wiggliness penalty} controls smoothness; $\lambda$ is estimated automatically
\item \textbf{REML} is the recommended criterion for smoothing parameter selection
\item \textbf{GAMs are GLMMs}: the penalized smooth decomposes into fixed + random effects
\item \textbf{glmmTMB} can fit GAMs alongside random effects in a unified framework
\end{itemize}

## End

\center

![](Thats_all_folks.png){width=75%}
